sources.certVol1: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

sourceAudit.note: Content is original/paraphrased from the cited sources; questions are original. Verify command syntax against official Cisco docs.

objectives.title: Configure and verify access control lists

ckus.title: Access Control Lists
ckus.summary: Ordered rules that permit/deny traffic by criteria. Processed top-down, first match wins, with an implicit deny-all at the end.

ckus.title: Standard ACL
ckus.summary: Matches SOURCE IP only (numbered 1–99/1300–1999). Place close to the destination.

ckus.title: Extended ACL
ckus.summary: Matches source + destination IP, protocol, and ports (numbered 100–199/2000–2699 or named). Place close to the source.

ckus.title: Wildcard Mask
ckus.summary: The inverse of a subnet mask used in ACLs: 0 = must match, 1 = don’t care. /24 → 0.0.0.255.

tiers.beginner: An ACL is a list of permit/deny rules a router checks against traffic. It reads the list top to bottom and stops at the first rule that matches. Anything not matched is denied by a hidden rule at the end. Standard ACLs only look at WHERE traffic came from; extended ACLs can also look at where it’s going and which application (port).
tiers.intermediate: ACLs filter traffic by criteria, processed top-down with first-match-wins and an implicit `deny any` at the end. Standard ACLs (1–99/1300–1999) match source IP only — place them close to the destination. Extended ACLs (100–199/2000–2699 or named) match source/destination IP, protocol, and ports — place them close to the source to drop unwanted traffic early. ACLs use wildcard masks (inverse of subnet masks: 0 = match, 1 = ignore). Apply to an interface with `ip access-group <name|number> in|out`.
tiers.examReady: ACL = ordered permit/deny rules; top-down, first match wins, implicit `deny any` at the end (so an all-deny ACL blocks everything). Standard (1–99/1300–1999): source IP only → place near the DESTINATION. Extended (100–199/2000–2699 / named): source+dest IP, protocol, ports → place near the SOURCE. Wildcard mask = inverse subnet mask (0 match / 1 don’t-care; host = 0.0.0.0, any = 255.255.255.255). Apply: `ip access-group 100 in` on an interface. Named ACLs allow editing by sequence number. Verify: `show access-lists`, `show ip interface`.

reading.definition: An **ACL** is an ordered list of permit/deny rules: **top-down, first match wins**, with an **implicit deny-all** at the end. **Standard** = source only (place near destination); **Extended** = source/dest/protocol/ports (place near source). ACLs use **wildcard masks**.
keyPoints: Top-down, first-match-wins, implicit `deny any` at the end.
Standard (1–99): source IP only → place near the destination.
Extended (100–199): src/dst IP, protocol, ports → place near the source.
Apply: `ip access-group <name|num> in|out` on an interface.
reading.realWorld: Because of the implicit deny, an ACL with only `permit` lines silently blocks everything else — always confirm needed traffic is permitted. Named ACLs let you insert/remove a single line by sequence number without rewriting the whole list.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the implicit `deny any` at the end.
Placing an extended ACL far from the source (wastes bandwidth).
Using a subnet mask instead of a wildcard mask.
Wrong rule order — a broad permit above a specific deny is never reached.
reading.advanced: Apply an ACL to vty lines with `access-class` to restrict management access. Extended ACLs can match TCP flags (e.g. `established`). Order matters: most-specific rules first.

questions.question: How are ACL entries processed?
questions.explanation: ACLs are read top-down and stop at the first match.

questions.question: True or False: An ACL has an implicit deny-all at the end.
questions.explanation: True — anything not explicitly permitted is denied.

questions.question: Where should a standard ACL be placed?
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs match source only, so place them near the destination to avoid over-blocking.

questions.question: Where should an extended ACL be placed?
questions.explanation: Extended ACLs are specific, so place near the source to drop traffic early.

questions.question: What wildcard mask matches exactly the /24 network 192.168.1.0?
Which number range is a standard ACL?
What can an extended ACL match that a standard ACL cannot?
questions.explanation: Extended ACLs match source+destination IP, protocol, and ports.

questions.question: Which command applies ACL 100 inbound on an interface?
questions.explanation: `ip access-group 100 in` applies the ACL to the interface.

questions.question: A specific `deny` never takes effect because a broad `permit` sits above it. Why?
choices: First match wins — the permit matches first
questions.explanation: Top-down first-match means the earlier broad permit matches before the specific deny.

flashcards.back: Top-down, first match wins, with an implicit deny-all at the end.

flashcards.front: Standard ACL: matches what, placed where?
flashcards.back: Source IP only; place near the destination. Numbers 1–99/1300–1999.

flashcards.front: Extended ACL: matches what, placed where?
flashcards.back: Src/dst IP, protocol, ports; place near the source. Numbers 100–199/2000–2699.
Inverse subnet mask: 0 = must match, 1 = don’t care. /24 → 0.0.0.255.

flashcards.front: Command to apply an ACL to an interface?
Wildcard for a single host? For any?

commands.purpose: Create a numbered standard ACL entry (source only).
Apply an ACL to an interface in a direction.

glossary.definition: Access Control List — ordered permit/deny rules for filtering traffic.
Matches source IP only; placed near the destination.
Matches src/dst IP, protocol, and ports; placed near the source.
Inverse subnet mask used in ACLs (0 = match, 1 = ignore).
The unseen deny-all rule at the end of every ACL.

mnemonics.title: Placement
mnemonics.mnemonic: Standard = near Destination; Extended = near Source.
mnemonics.explanation: Standard/destination and Extended/source — place each where it limits damage.

examTraps.correction: An ACL with only permits blocks everything else via the implicit deny any.

examTraps.trap: Using a subnet mask in an ACL.

examTraps.correction: First match wins — put specific rules before broad ones.

examTraps.trap: Placing extended ACLs near the destination.
examTraps.correction: Place extended ACLs near the source to drop traffic early.

misconceptions.misconception: ACLs encrypt or deeply inspect traffic.
misconceptions.reality: ACLs filter by header fields (IP/protocol/port); they are not encryption or stateful firewalls.
misconceptions.example: An ACL permits TCP/443 but does not inspect the TLS payload.

misconceptions.reality: Order is critical — the first matching rule wins and the rest are skipped.
misconceptions.example: A broad permit above a specific deny makes the deny dead.

diagram.title: ACL first-match processing
annotations: First matching rule decides and processing stops.
No match anywhere → implicit deny any.

packetFlow.title: Evaluating a packet against an ACL

steps.title: Top of list
steps.action: Start at the first ACL entry.

steps.title: Compare
steps.action: Check the packet against the entry’s criteria.

steps.title: First match
steps.action: On a match, apply permit/deny and STOP.

steps.title: Implicit deny
steps.action: If no entry matched, the implicit deny any drops it.

ckus.title: CIA Triad
ckus.summary: Confidentiality (encryption, access control), Integrity (hashing/checksums detect unauthorized changes), Availability (redundancy, DDoS mitigation). Every security control maps to one or more of these goals.

ckus.title: Vulnerability, Threat, Exploit
ckus.summary: Vulnerability = a weakness (unpatched software, misconfiguration). Threat = a potential danger that could exploit a vulnerability. Exploit = the actual code/method used. Risk = likelihood x impact.

ckus.title: Common Network Threats
ckus.summary: Malware (virus, worm, Trojan, ransomware), Phishing/social engineering, DoS/DDoS (overwhelm availability), Man-in-the-middle (intercept/modify traffic), Spoofing (fake IP/MAC), Password attacks (brute force, dictionary).

ckus.title: Mitigation Techniques
ckus.summary: Patching removes known vulnerabilities. Firewalls/ACLs filter traffic. Network segmentation limits blast radius. End-user security awareness training addresses social engineering. Defense in depth = multiple overlapping controls.

tiers.beginner: Network security has three goals: keep data private (confidentiality), ensure it is not changed without detection (integrity), and keep systems running (availability) — called the CIA triad. Common threats include ransomware, phishing emails, and floods that take a network offline. Defenses include patching software, firewalls, and training users.
tiers.intermediate: CIA triad: Confidentiality = encrypt data and restrict access; Integrity = use hashing (MD5, SHA) to detect unauthorized changes; Availability = redundancy, DDoS protection. Key terminology: vulnerability (weakness — unpatched OS), threat (potential danger), exploit (actual attack code), risk (likelihood x impact). Malware types: virus (file-attached, requires user action to spread), worm (self-propagates without files), Trojan (appears legitimate), ransomware (encrypts and demands payment). Social engineering: phishing (email), vishing (voice), smishing (SMS). DoS/DDoS floods exhaust resources. MITM intercepts/alters traffic. Spoofing fakes source identity.
tiers.examReady: CIA: Confidentiality = encryption (AES, RSA), access control; Integrity = hashing (SHA-256, MD5 deprecated); Availability = FHRP redundancy, DDoS mitigation. Vulnerability vs threat vs exploit: gap vs actor/event vs weapon. Malware: virus (needs host file), worm (self-replicating, no file), Trojan (disguised), ransomware (encrypts + demands payment). Social engineering: phishing/vishing/smishing/tailgating. DoS = single attacker; DDoS = distributed botnet. MITM intercepts and possibly modifies traffic — stopped by encryption (TLS) and certificate validation. Spoofing: IP spoofing (fake src IP), ARP spoofing (fake MAC), DNS spoofing (fake resolution). Mitigations: patching, NGFW, ACLs, port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, AAA, segmentation, user training. Defense in depth = multiple layers.

reading.definition: The **CIA triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) frames all security decisions. A **vulnerability** is a weakness; a **threat** is the potential to exploit it; an **exploit** is the actual attack. Defense in depth uses multiple overlapping controls.
keyPoints: CIA: **C**=encryption+access control, **I**=hashing (integrity of data), **A**=redundancy+DDoS (keep systems running).
Vulnerability (gap) vs threat (potential danger) vs exploit (actual attack) — know the distinction.
Malware: virus (file), worm (no file, self-replicates), Trojan (disguised), ransomware (encrypts+demands payment).
Social engineering / phishing = exploiting human trust — the most common initial attack vector.
Defense in depth: layer multiple controls so no single failure is catastrophic.
reading.realWorld: A ransomware attack chain: vulnerability (unpatched OS) + threat (ransomware group) + exploit (phishing email attachment) = breach. Mitigations: patch the OS, train users to spot phishing, back up data offline, segment the network to limit spread.
commonMistakes: Confusing integrity with access control. Integrity = detecting unauthorized CHANGES (hashing). Confidentiality = keeping data private.
Thinking a vulnerability alone = a breach. It needs a threat + exploit to become a real risk.
Forgetting availability is a security goal — DDoS attacks violate it without touching confidentiality or integrity.
reading.advanced: Zero-day = exploit for an unknown or unpatched vulnerability. APT (advanced persistent threat) = sophisticated, long-term attacker. Insider threats come from trusted users. Tailgating = physical social engineering (following someone through a door).

questions.question: Encryption of data in transit primarily supports which CIA triad element?
questions.explanation: Encryption protects confidentiality. Integrity is addressed by hashing; availability by redundancy.

questions.question: Hashing a file to detect unauthorized modifications primarily addresses which CIA element?
questions.explanation: Integrity ensures data has not been altered without detection. Hashing creates a fingerprint — if the hash changes, the data was modified.

questions.question: An unpatched web server running software with a known security flaw is best described as a:
questions.explanation: A vulnerability is a weakness (unpatched software). A threat is a potential danger. An exploit is the actual attack.

questions.question: Which malware type encrypts files and demands payment for the decryption key?
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts files and demands a ransom. A virus attaches to files; a worm self-propagates; a Trojan disguises itself as legitimate software.

questions.question: An attacker sends a fraudulent email appearing to be from IT, asking users to click a link and enter credentials. This is:
questions.explanation: Phishing is social engineering via deceptive email to trick users into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links.

questions.question: A website is flooded with millions of requests from a botnet, making it unavailable. Which CIA element is violated?
questions.explanation: A DDoS attack violates availability — legitimate users cannot access services because resources are exhausted.

questions.question: Using firewalls, user training, antivirus, and network segmentation together is called:
questions.explanation: Defense in depth uses multiple overlapping controls so that if one layer fails, others still protect. No single control is sufficient.

questions.question: An attacker intercepts traffic between two hosts and reads (or modifies) it without either host knowing. This is:
questions.explanation: A man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack positions the attacker between two communicating parties to eavesdrop or alter traffic. TLS encryption mitigates this.

questions.question: Which malware self-replicates across networks WITHOUT needing to attach to an existing file?
questions.explanation: A worm self-propagates without a host file — it spreads by exploiting vulnerabilities. A virus requires attaching to a file.

questions.question: The most effective mitigation against phishing and social engineering is:
questions.explanation: Social engineering exploits human trust. User security awareness training is the primary defense — teaching people to recognize and report suspicious emails and requests.

flashcards.front: CIA triad — what does each letter stand for?
flashcards.back: C=Confidentiality (encryption, access control), I=Integrity (hashing), A=Availability (redundancy, DDoS protection).

flashcards.front: Which CIA element does hashing protect?
flashcards.back: Integrity — a hash detects unauthorized changes. Encryption protects confidentiality; redundancy protects availability.
Vulnerability = weakness. Threat = potential danger. Exploit = actual attack method. Risk = likelihood x impact.
Virus = needs a host file. Worm = self-propagates without file. Trojan = disguised. Ransomware = encrypts + demands payment.
Social engineering via deceptive email to steal credentials or deliver malware. Vishing=voice, smishing=SMS.
Multiple overlapping security layers (firewall + ACL + training + endpoint protection) so a single failure does not compromise everything.

diagram.title: CIA Triad
annotations: C: keep data private from unauthorized parties

glossary.definition: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three foundational security goals.
A weakness that could be exploited (unpatched software, misconfiguration).
Layered security controls so one failure does not compromise the entire network.

mnemonics.title: CIA triad
mnemonics.mnemonic: Keep Secrets (C), Catch Changes (I), Stay Online (A).

misconceptions.reality: Integrity detects unauthorized changes (hashing); confidentiality keeps data private (encryption).
misconceptions.example: SHA-256 hash verifies file integrity, not secrecy.

misconceptions.misconception: A vulnerability alone equals a breach.
misconceptions.reality: A threat and exploit are also required — vulnerability is only the weakness.
misconceptions.example: Unpatched server is vulnerable; attacker must actually exploit it.

steps.title: Vulnerability
steps.action: Identify weakness (unpatched OS, open port).

steps.title: Threat
steps.action: Actor or event that could exploit the weakness.

steps.title: Exploit
steps.action: Actual attack method or code used.

steps.title: Mitigate
steps.action: Apply defense in depth: patch, ACL, training, segmentation.

examTraps.trap: Integrity = detecting CHANGES (hashing). NOT keeping data private.
examTraps.correction: That is confidentiality. Integrity uses hashing to detect unauthorized modification.

examTraps.trap: A DDoS attack violates AVAILABILITY, not confidentiality or integrity.
examTraps.correction: Availability is a security goal — DDoS exhausts resources so legitimate users cannot access services.

lab.title: Configure Dynamic ARP Inspection with DHCP Snooping
lab.scenario: SW1 must protect the LAN from forged ARP traffic. You will enable DHCP Snooping first so the switch builds a trusted IP-to-MAC binding table, then turn on Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) so valid DHCP clients are allowed while an attacker using a static IP (no snooping binding) is blocked.
learningGoals: Explain why DAI depends on the DHCP Snooping binding table.
Configure DHCP Snooping globally, per-VLAN, and trust the server-facing port.
Enable DAI on a VLAN and trust the correct ports.
Verify that a static-IP attacker with no binding is dropped.

tasks.title: Hostname & router IP
tasks.instruction: Set R1’s hostname and configure Gi0/0 with 192.168.10.254/24 (the gateway hosts will use).

tasks.title: DHCP pool on R1
tasks.instruction: Create a DHCP pool so valid clients receive an address (this is what populates the snooping binding table).

tasks.title: Access ports on SW1
tasks.instruction: Set SW1 ports Gi0/0 (attacker), Gi0/1 (valid PC), and Gi0/2 (to R1) as access ports in VLAN 1.

tasks.title: Enable DHCP Snooping
tasks.instruction: Enable DHCP Snooping globally and on VLAN 1, then TRUST only the server-facing uplink Gi0/2.

tasks.title: Enable Dynamic ARP Inspection
tasks.instruction: Enable DAI on VLAN 1 and trust the uplink Gi0/2 (and the valid host port Gi0/1) so only the attacker port stays untrusted.

tasks.title: Attacker test
tasks.instruction: Assign the attacker a STATIC 192.168.10.2/24 (no DHCP, so no snooping binding) and ping the gateway — it must fail.

successCriteria: The valid PC receives a DHCP address from R1.
SW1 shows a DHCP Snooping binding for the valid PC.
SW1 logs invalid/denied ARP entries for the attacker port.
failureCriteria: DAI enabled before DHCP Snooping has a working binding table → valid hosts dropped too.
DHCP Snooping not enabled on the VLAN → no bindings, everything fails DAI.
Attacker-facing port trusted → forged ARP is allowed through.
All ports trusted → DAI does nothing.
commonMistakes: Trusting the attacker-facing port (Gi0/0) — it must stay untrusted.
Forgetting `ip dhcp snooping vlan 1`, so no bindings are built.
Enabling DAI before DHCP Snooping is actually issuing leases.
Expecting a static-IP host to pass DAI without a binding or an ARP ACL.
Trusting every port, which disables the protection.

topology.title: DAI topology
notes: Only the uplink (and valid host) ports are trusted; the attacker port is untrusted.

verificationChecks.expectedResult: IP-MAC-port binding for the valid PC on Gi0/1.
DAI enabled on VLAN 1; untrusted ports listed.

failureChecks.reason: No DHCP snooping binding exists for the static IP, so DAI drops its ARP.

failureChecks.expectedFailure: Invalid ARP log entries on the attacker port.
failureChecks.reason: DAI logs and drops the forged ARP packets.

diagram.title: DAI drops the static-IP attacker
annotations: The attacker’s static .2 has no DHCP Snooping binding.
DAI compares the ARP against the binding table, finds no match, and drops + logs it.

steps.title: DHCP lease
steps.action: Valid PC gets 192.168.10.x via DHCP through the trusted uplink.

steps.title: Binding stored
steps.action: DHCP Snooping records IP-MAC-port in the binding table.

steps.title: ARP check
steps.action: PC’s ARP matches its binding, so DAI permits it.

steps.title: Forward
steps.action: ARP is forwarded; the PC reaches the gateway.

steps.title: Static IP
steps.action: Attacker sets a static 192.168.10.2 — no DHCP, no binding.

steps.title: ARP sent
steps.action: Attacker sends ARP on the untrusted port.

steps.title: No binding
steps.action: DAI finds no matching binding in the snooping table.

steps.title: Drop + log
steps.action: Switch drops the ARP and logs an invalid-ARP entry.

lab.title: Configure Standard and Extended ACLs
lab.scenario: R1 connects an office subnet (192.168.1.0/24) to a server zone (10.0.0.0/24) and the internet. Configure a standard numbered ACL to block one host from the internet, then an extended named ACL to permit HTTP/HTTPS from the office to the servers while blocking all other office-to-server traffic.
learningGoals: Configure a standard numbered ACL and apply it close to the destination.
Configure an extended named ACL and apply it close to the source.
Understand wildcard masks and the implicit deny at the end of every ACL.
Verify with show access-lists and show ip interface.

tasks.title: Configure interfaces
tasks.instruction: Configure R1 Gi0/0 as 192.168.1.1/24 (office) and Gi0/1 as 10.0.0.1/24 (server zone). Bring both interfaces up.

tasks.title: Standard ACL — block host 192.168.1.50 outbound
tasks.instruction: Create standard ACL 10: deny 192.168.1.50, then permit 192.168.1.0/24. Apply OUTBOUND on Gi0/2 (internet-facing).

tasks.title: Named extended ACL
tasks.instruction: Create named extended ACL OFFICE_TO_SERVERS: permit TCP from 192.168.1.0/24 to 10.0.0.0/24 on ports 80 and 443; deny ip from office to servers. Apply INBOUND on Gi0/0.

tasks.title: Verify ACLs
tasks.instruction: Verify both ACLs are configured and show hit counts.

successCriteria: ACL 10 blocks only 192.168.1.50 from reaching the internet.
Other 192.168.1.0/24 hosts can reach the internet.
OFFICE_TO_SERVERS permits port 80 and 443 from office to servers.
Non-HTTP/HTTPS traffic from office is blocked from server zone.
show access-lists shows match counters incrementing on the correct entries.
failureCriteria: Standard ACL on Gi0/0 inbound — blocks more traffic than intended.
Missing permit after deny — implicit deny drops all remaining traffic.
Subnet mask used instead of wildcard mask — ACL matches wrong addresses.
ACL applied on wrong interface or wrong direction.
commonMistakes: Confusing subnet mask with wildcard mask — wildcard is bitwise inverse (255 - mask octet).
Placing standard ACL close to source — standard ACLs should go close to destination.
Forgetting explicit permit after deny — implicit deny at end drops everything else.
Wrong port keyword — eq 80=HTTP, eq 443=HTTPS, eq 23=Telnet, eq 22=SSH.

verificationChecks.expectedResult: Both ACL 10 and OFFICE_TO_SERVERS listed with rules.

failureChecks.reason: Telnet (TCP/23) is not in the permitted ports in OFFICE_TO_SERVERS.

diagram.title: Standard vs Extended ACL Placement
annotations: Standard ACL: source IP only — place near DESTINATION to avoid over-blocking
Extended ACL: src+dst+protocol+port — place near SOURCE to save bandwidth

packetFlows.title: HTTP traffic from office to server

steps.title: Packet arrives Gi0/0
steps.action: PC sends HTTP request to 10.0.0.10. Packet arrives inbound on R1 Gi0/0.

steps.title: ACL checked
steps.action: OFFICE_TO_SERVERS inbound. First matching rule: permit tcp ... eq 80. Match!

steps.title: Forwarded
steps.action: Packet is permitted and forwarded out Gi0/1 to the server.

lab.title: Harden Device Access (Console and Enable)
lab.scenario: Secure R1: set encrypted enable secret, create a local admin user, and require local login on the console line.

tasks.title: Enable secret
tasks.instruction: Set the enable secret to ciscoenable (encrypted).

tasks.title: Local user
tasks.instruction: Create username admin privilege 15 secret adminpass.

tasks.title: Console login
tasks.instruction: On line con 0, require login local.

tasks.title: Verify config
tasks.instruction: Review the running config for login settings.

commonMistakes: Using enable password instead of enable secret
login without local when using username

steps.title: Login
steps.action: Console prompts for username/secret

lab.title: Configure Local AAA Authentication
lab.scenario: Enable AAA new-model on R1 and use the local database for VTY login authentication (simplified alternative to TACACS+ when no server is available).
learningGoals: Contrast with TACACS+ for centralized auth

tasks.title: Local user
tasks.instruction: Create user netadmin privilege 15 secret NetPass123.

tasks.title: Enable AAA
tasks.instruction: Enable AAA new model.

tasks.title: Login method list
tasks.instruction: Set default login authentication to use the local database.

tasks.title: VTY login
tasks.instruction: Apply login authentication on VTY lines 0-4.

tasks.title: Verify
tasks.instruction: Show AAA configuration.

failureCriteria: aaa new-model without login list — VTY may break
commonMistakes: Forgetting username before switching to aaa authentication login default local

steps.title: Auth
steps.action: AAA delegates to local username database

lab.title: Troubleshoot ACL Blocking Return Traffic
lab.scenario: Symptom: Office PC can initiate ping to server 10.0.0.10 but gets no reply. An extended ACL OFFICE_TO_SERVERS on Gi0/0 IN permits icmp out but has an explicit deny ip any any at the end, blocking return traffic. Add permit ip any any after the icmp permit (before implicit deny) — or replace with permit ip 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255.
learningGoals: Use show commands to isolate fault

tasks.title: Inspect ACL hits
tasks.instruction: show access-lists — note deny counters incrementing on return traffic.

tasks.title: Fix ACL
tasks.instruction: Inside OFFICE_TO_SERVERS, add: permit ip 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255

tasks.title: Retest
tasks.instruction: Ping 10.0.0.10 again.

successCriteria: Symptom resolved after fix commands entered
failureCriteria: Fix applied on wrong interface or wrong direction
commonMistakes: Moving ACL to wrong interface instead of fixing entries

steps.title: Symptom
steps.action: Symptom: Office PC can initiate ping to server 10.0.0.10 but gets no reply. An e

steps.title: Fix
steps.action: Correct command restores expected behavior

lab.title: Configure Port Security on an Access Port
lab.scenario: SW1 Fa0/5 connects a single corporate PC. Limit the port to one MAC address, enable sticky learning, and set violation mode to shutdown.

tasks.title: Access VLAN
tasks.instruction: Set Fa0/5 as access port in VLAN 10.

tasks.title: Enable port-security
tasks.instruction: Enable port-security with max 1 MAC and sticky.

tasks.title: Violation mode
tasks.instruction: Set violation mode to shutdown (err-disable the port).

tasks.title: Verify
tasks.instruction: Confirm port-security is active on Fa0/5.

commonMistakes: Port-security on a trunk port
Forgetting sticky — MAC table clears on reload
restrict mode vs shutdown confusion

topology.title: Configure Port Security on an Access Port

diagram.title: Configure Port Security on an Access Port

packetFlows.title: Configure Port Security on an Access Port

steps.title: Learn
steps.action: First host MAC learned as sticky secure address

steps.title: Violate
steps.action: Second MAC triggers shutdown violation

lab.title: Build and Apply an Extended Named ACL
lab.scenario: R1 connects office 192.168.1.0/24 (Gi0/0) to servers 10.0.0.0/24 (Gi0/1). Create named ACL WEB_ONLY permitting HTTP/HTTPS from office to servers, deny other office-to-server IP, apply inbound on Gi0/0.

tasks.title: Create ACL
tasks.instruction: Create extended ACL WEB_ONLY with permit tcp 80 and 443 from office to servers.

tasks.title: Explicit deny
tasks.instruction: Add deny ip from office to server subnet, then end ACL.

tasks.title: Apply inbound
tasks.instruction: Apply WEB_ONLY inbound on Gi0/0 (office-facing).

tasks.title: Verify
tasks.instruction: Show ACL entries and hit counts.

commonMistakes: Subnet mask instead of wildcard
Applying outbound on server interface for source filtering
Missing deny — relies only on implicit deny for wrong protocols

topology.title: Build and Apply an Extended Named ACL

diagram.title: Build and Apply an Extended Named ACL

packetFlows.title: Build and Apply an Extended Named ACL

steps.title: Match permit
steps.action: TCP 80/443 matches first permit lines

steps.title: Deny other
steps.action: Other IP traffic hits explicit deny
